title: rcicrely user guide
text: scaling underlying mask. Pearson r per column exactly 1 (linear scaling correlation- preserving), row-mean variance ICC formula depends ~25x larger rendered version. ICC(3,*) computed rendered data therefore ICC computed raw mask. logic applies Euclidean distance infoVal.
code: set.seed(1) n_pix <- 32L * 32L n_p   <- 20L mask  <- matrix(rnorm(n_pix * n_p, sd = 0.02), n_pix, n_p)  # Render: stretch each column to [-0.5, 0.5] (Schmitz "matched") rendered <- apply(mask, 2L, function(m) {   rng <- diff(range(m)); if (rng == 0) m else m * (1 / rng) })  c(   raw_max_range      = round(diff(range(mask)),     3L),   rendered_max_range = round(diff(range(rendered)), 3L) ) #>      raw_max_range rendered_max_range  #>              0.162              1.159  # Pearson r between same-column raw and rendered: ~ 1 # (a perfect linear stretch preserves Pearson) mean(vapply(seq_len(n_p),             function(j) cor(mask[, j], rendered[, j]),             numeric(1L))) #> [1] 1  # But the variance ratios that drive ICC are very different var(rowMeans(mask))      # variance of the group mean over pixels #> [1] 1.923774e-05 var(rowMeans(rendered))  # same metric, on the rendered version #> [1] 0.001134698

title: rcicrely user guide
text: 2IFC Brief-RC pipelines depend base face image. visible quality generated CIs reliability statistics follow sensitive image prepared. Requirements: Square (e.g. 256x256, 512x512). Grayscale — single channel. RGB images error rcicr non-conformable-array messages. Pixel range [0, 1] (convention png::readPNG jpeg::readJPEG produce). Centred face eye / nose / mouth roughly geometry Schmitz oval mask assumes (eyes near upper third, mouth near lower third). built-face_mask(region = ...) geometries assume layout; custom -centre faces need mask geometry. conventional base face morph several individual faces, removes idiosyncratic features centres geometry. webmorphR (DeBruine, 2022) current best--class tool producing one. Typical pipeline: write_stim() produces stimuli/base.png, ready rcicr::generateStimuli2IFC(base_face_files = list(base = "stimuli/base.png")). non-square crops, oval-region operations, feature-aligned masks (plug load_face_mask()), see ?webmorphR::mask_oval ?webmorphR::crop_tem. Cite DeBruine (2022). stack morph (e.g. working single base photograph), prepare hand: image editor (GIMP / Krita / Photoshop / PowerPoint export). Convert grayscale, crop square fits face within central ~70%, resize target dimension, save PNG.
code: # install.packages("webmorphR") library(webmorphR)  stim <- read_stim("path/to/raw_face_images/") |>   auto_delin() |>                       # auto landmark delineation   align(procrustes = TRUE) |>           # Procrustes alignment   crop(width = 0.85, height = 0.85) |>  # tight crop around the face   to_size(c(256, 256)) |>               # resize to rcicr-friendly size   greyscale() |>                        # single channel, in [0, 1]   avg()                                 # morph into one average face  write_stim(stim, dir = "stimuli/", names = "base", format = "png")

title: rcicrely user guide
text: One row per trial. format can data.frame, data.table, tibble, anything behaves like data frame; source (CSV, RData, Parquet, programmatic construction) matter, columns present. Response coding. 2IFC: response = +1 participant chose oriented variant trial, -1 inverted variant. trial unique stimulus pair, stimulus indexes pair ranges 1:n_trials. Brief-RC 12 (Schmitz, Rougier & Yzerbyt, 2024): trial participant sees 12 noisy faces (6 oriented + 6 inverted 6 distinct underlying noise patterns) picks one. Recorded one row per trial, stimulus = pool id chosen pattern response = +1 oriented variant chosen, -1 inverted. stimulus id can repeat across trials. Watch {0, 1} miscoding. single common silent failure RC pipelines: experiment software sometimes records “left” / “right” “first” / “second” 0 / 1, analyst forgets recode. Pass {0, 1} responses ci_from_responses_*() resulting CI approximately blank — math leans heavily sign. functions error input boundary responses {-1, +1}. see error, fix one line: responses$response <- 2 * responses$response - 1. Brief-RC: record 11 unselected faces. Brief-RC trial shows 12 faces participant chooses one. Record chosen face (one row per trial, ). record 11 unselected faces additional rows response = 0, use “expanded” 12-rows-per-trial format weights chosen +1 unchosen -1/11. Neither convention matches Schmitz genMask() formula (mask = noise[, chosen] %*% chosen_responses / n_unique_chosen) ci_from_responses_briefrc() implements. Unselected faces’ noise patterns simply enter multiplication; absence zero.

code: set.seed(1) make_condition <- function(region_top, n_pix = 32L * 32L,                            n_p = 30L, snr = 0.3) {   noise <- matrix(rnorm(n_pix * n_p, sd = 0.05), n_pix, n_p)   mask <- if (region_top) {     c(rep(1, n_pix / 2L), rep(0, n_pix / 2L))   } else {     c(rep(0, n_pix / 2L), rep(1, n_pix / 2L))   }   out <- noise + snr * outer(mask, runif(n_p, 0.7, 1.3))   attr(out, "img_dims") <- c(32L, 32L)   out } sig_A <- make_condition(region_top = TRUE,  n_p = 30L) sig_B <- make_condition(region_top = FALSE, n_p = 30L)

Permutation distribution split-half r. Reading result. $r_hh = mean per-split r. $r_sb = Spearman-Brown projected reliability full sample (report unless asked otherwise). $ci_95, $ci_95_sb percentile CIs. $distribution full per-permutation r vector. Common mistakes. Reporting $r_hh instead $r_sb audience cares full-sample CI. Running n_permutations < 200 trusting CIs. Real example (Oliveira et al., 2019, Study 1). code: r_sh <- rel_split_half(sig_A, n_permutations = 500L, seed = 1L,                        progress = FALSE) print(r_sh) #> <rcicrely split-half reliability> #>   N producers:          30 #>   n_permutations:       500 #>   mean per-split r:     0.993  [0.992, 0.993] #>   Spearman-Brown r_SB:  0.996  [0.996, 0.997] plot(r_sh) # sm_trust, sm_dominant: pixels x 20 producer signal matrices # (built from the response data; see study1_replication.Rmd) rel_split_half(sm_trust,    n_permutations = 500L, seed = 1L) #> Trust:    r_hh = 0.405, r_sb = 0.577, 95% CI on r_sb = [0.539, 0.616] rel_split_half(sm_dominant, n_permutations = 500L, seed = 1L) #> Dominant: r_hh = 0.262, r_sb = 0.415, 95% CI on r_sb = [0.380, 0.450]

what: 5.2 rel_loo() - influence screening (not a reliability metric)
title: rcicrely user guide
text: . producer, correlates full-sample group CI group CI computed without producer, returns z-scored version correlations. Producers whose z-score sits clearly zero disproportionate influence group pattern flagged inspection. use . screen producers whose individual CI far group pattern - often sign response miscoding, fatigue, genuinely atypical mental representation. diagnostic; reliability statistic (see note end section). default mean +/- SD; use flag_method = "mad" median +/- MAD rule doesn’t get pulled around outliers tries detect: z-scored LOO influence per producer. tidy ordered table producers influence, use rel_loo_z(): Reading result. $z_scores informative quantity - plot / report directly. $correlations retained transparency (values near 1 construction full-sample mean leave-one-mean share (N - 1) / N data). $flagged lists ids threshold. $summary_df tidy table sorted z_score. read $correlations reliability. r_loo values almost always [0.95, 0.999] N = 30, even noisy CIs, two vectors correlated overlap (N - 1) / N data. naive reader can misinterpret “LOO r = 0.98” “CIs 98% reliable”; wrong. Report reliability via rel_split_half() rel_icc() (§5.1, §5.3). Common mistakes. Treating $flagged “drop producers” - investigate first. flagged producer may genuinely atypical mental representation rather bad data. Cross-check rcicrdiagnostics rule response-coding errors. Real example (Oliveira et al., 2019, Study 1, Trust condition). Median raw r_loo .992 (MAD = .001) — illustrating near-1 absolute value structurally. Zero producers cleared default MAD-based flag threshold, z-scored ordering still informative inspection priority: producers top z-ordering ones whose removal moves group CI relative rest — even none clear flag threshold, inspection order.
code: r_loo <- rel_loo(sig_A, flag_threshold = 2.5, flag_method = "sd") print(r_loo) #> <rcicrely leave-one-out influence screening> #>   N producers:        30 #>   flag rule:          sd (threshold = 2.50) #>  #>   z-scored influence (most influential first): #>     p002                  z =  -2.19  (r_loo = 0.9999) #>     p029                  z =  -1.65  (r_loo = 0.9999) #>     p003                  z =  -1.58  (r_loo = 0.9999) #>     p001                  z =  -1.43  (r_loo = 0.9999) #>     p026                  z =  -0.88  (r_loo = 0.9999) #>     ... (25 more) #>  #>   flagged producers:  none #>  #>   Note: r_loo values are near 1 by construction because the #>   full-sample and leave-one-out means share (N-1)/N of their #>   data. Use z_scores (relative ordering), not r_loo levels, #>   to interpret influence. This is a diagnostic, not a #>   reliability statistic. r_loo_mad <- rel_loo(sig_A, flag_threshold = 2.5, flag_method = "mad") c(sd_threshold = r_loo$threshold, mad_threshold = r_loo_mad$threshold) #>  sd_threshold mad_threshold  #>     0.9999303     0.9999308 plot(r_loo) head(rel_loo_z(r_loo)) #   participant_id  correlation  z_score  flag # 1     outlier01        0.9714    -3.12  TRUE # 2           p07        0.9942    -0.84  FALSE # ... loo <- rel_loo(sm_trust, flag_method = "mad") head(rel_loo_z(loo), 3) #>   participant_id  correlation  z_score  flag #> 1          18043       0.9905    -1.68  FALSE #> 2          18051       0.9911    -0.99  FALSE #> 3          18131       0.9913    -0.79  FALSE

title: rcicrely user guide
text: . Two-way mixed ICC via direct mean squares. Default returns ICC(3,1) ICC(3,k). Pixels fixed (image grid), participants random (drawn producer population). use . Single-scalar reliability summary. ICC(3,k) “stable group-mean CI?”, ICC(3,1) “informative one producer’s CI?”. also report ICC(2,*) reviewer expects : Reading result. $icc_3_1, $icc_3_k, $icc_2_1, $icc_2_k (latter two NA unless requested). $ms_rows, $ms_cols, $ms_error underlying ANOVA mean squares transparency. $model description string print method shows. Common mistakes. Asking ICC(2,) reviewer expects . ICC(2,) treats pixels random sample pixel population, aren’t. numbers usually close ICC(3,*) high pixel counts model mis-specified - report variants = c("3_1", "3_k", "2_1", "2_k") asked. ICC strongly scale-sensitive. Use raw mask . Real example (Oliveira et al., 2019, Study 1). Two-way mixed ICC 65,536-pixel raw masks, 20 producers per condition: ICC(3,1) — resolution-comparable single-producer reliability — clearly higher Trust Dominant, matching reliability ordering reported original paper. ICC(3,k) high absolute terms resolution asymptote known property ICC(*,k) large pixel counts; report ICC(3,1) primary cross-study comparable number.
code: r_icc <- rel_icc(sig_A, variants = c("3_1", "3_k")) print(r_icc) #> <rcicrely ICC> #>   model:        ICC(3,*) / two-way mixed; pixels fixed, participants random #>   N targets:    1024 pixels #>   N raters:     30 participants #>  #>   Primary: ICC(3,1) (single-producer reliability) #>   ICC(3,1):      0.8788 #>  #>   Secondary: ICC(3,k) (group-mean reliability) #>   ICC(3,k):      0.9954 r_icc_full <- rel_icc(sig_A, variants = c("3_1", "3_k", "2_1", "2_k")) print(r_icc_full) #> <rcicrely ICC> #>   model:        ICC(3,*) / two-way mixed; pixels fixed, participants random #>   N targets:    1024 pixels #>   N raters:     30 participants #>  #>   Primary: ICC(3,1) (single-producer reliability) #>   ICC(3,1):      0.8788 #>  #>   Secondary: ICC(3,k) (group-mean reliability) #>   ICC(3,k):      0.9954 #>  #>   Two-way-random variants (for reviewer comparability only): #>   ICC(2,1):      0.8565 #>   ICC(2,k):      0.9944 rel_icc(sm_trust) #> ICC(3,1) = 0.1008,  ICC(3,k) = 0.6917 rel_icc(sm_dominant) #> ICC(3,1) = 0.0489,  ICC(3,k) = 0.5068

text: two examples §5 (sig_A, sig_B) use half-image signal, produces strong contrasts — useful demonstrating within-condition reliability metrics work, makes -condition cluster figures look like two solid blobs rather realistic localized clusters. §6.1–§6.2 therefore use small additional fixture localized circular signal: two small “patches” different positions 32 x 32 grid, mimicking real RC signal tends cluster around face features rather fill whole image.
code: set.seed(7) make_localized <- function(centre, n_p = 30L, n_side = 32L,                            snr = 0.20, radius = 0.18, seed = NULL) {   if (!is.null(seed)) set.seed(seed)   rr <- row(matrix(0, n_side, n_side)) / n_side - centre[1]   cc <- col(matrix(0, n_side, n_side)) / n_side - centre[2]   d  <- sqrt(rr^2 + cc^2)   m  <- as.vector(pmax(0, 1 - d / radius))   out <- snr * outer(m, runif(n_p, 0.7, 1.3)) +          matrix(rnorm(n_side^2 * n_p, sd = 0.05), n_side^2, n_p)   attr(out, "img_dims") <- c(n_side, n_side)   out } # Condition A: signal in upper-left; Condition B: signal in lower-right sig_A_loc <- make_localized(c(0.30, 0.30), n_p = 30L, seed = 11L) sig_B_loc <- make_localized(c(0.70, 0.70), n_p = 30L, seed = 12L)

what: 6.2 rel_cluster_test() — spatially-localized inference with FWER control
title: rcicrely user guide
text: . Identifies spatially contiguous regions image two conditions differ, family-wise error control across whole image (-claim significance kind mass-testing problem §6.1 warned ). vocabulary. Three terms unavoidable; one means plain language. Cluster-forming threshold (cluster_threshold, default 2.0). t-value cutoff used first step: pixel |t| > 2.0 “”, everything else “”. significance threshold — cutoff deciding pixels even candidates part cluster. Significance decided afterwards. Lower thresholds catch diffuse signal; higher thresholds isolate sharper peaks. 4-connectivity. binary “/ ” image, group adjacent “” pixels clusters. 4-connectivity treats two pixels adjacent share edge (/ / left / right) — four orthogonal neighbours. 8-connectivity, alternative, also counts diagonals. 4-connectivity conservative choice: one-pixel-wide diagonal stripe split isolated pixels forms cluster, whereas 8-connectivity chain . Suits face-CI signal, tends contiguous orthogonal directions. Cluster mass (mass). candidate cluster, mass sum t-values within . pixel count. means small cluster strong t-values can mass large cluster weak t-values — metric mixes extent (big) magnitude (strong). Cluster mass introduced Maris & Oostenveld (2007) dominant choice EEG / MEG / fMRI cluster-permutation work. Stratified label permutation. build null distribution, randomly relabel producers belong vs. B many times, recompute t-map, find clusters, record maximum cluster mass per permutation. “Stratified” means preserve original group sizes (N_A, N_B) exactly time, Welch denominator perturbed H0. paired = TRUE, permutation sign-flip matched pairs instead. Max-statistic FWER. Family-wise error rate = probability false positive across entire image. comparing observed cluster mass maximum mass permutation, directly bound probability alpha. per-pixel correction needed; cluster-level p-value already FWE-corrected. use . “image two conditions differ, confident differences noise?” Pixel-wise Welch t-map (red = condition larger, blue = condition B larger) significant cluster contours overlaid black. two clusters correspond two injected signal patches, clusters FWE-corrected significant alpha = 0.05; surrounding noise produce false-positive clusters thanks max-statistic correction. Reading result. $observed_t raw t-map. $clusters data.frame one row per candidate cluster, columns: cluster_id, direction ("pos"/"neg"), mass (sum t-values), size (pixel count), p_value (mass percentile null), significant (logical). $pos_labels / $neg_labels integer matrices shape image, non-zero value tags cluster — useful custom plotting overlays. $null_distribution exposes per-permutation max masses. TFCE alternative. threshold-free cluster enhancement, integrates across many cluster-forming thresholds removes arbitrary cutoff choice, set method = "tfce". See §6.5 deep-dive tutorial full mathematical detail §6.7 agreement-map relative. TFCE-enhanced map (cluster-forming threshold choose). Significant pixels outlined black. Compare threshold-based version — TFCE generally power diffuse, low-amplitude signal integrates evidence across many thresholds rather committing one. Common mistakes. Reading cluster_threshold = 2.0 “p < 0.05”. t-cutoff forming candidate clusters, cluster significance threshold. Trusting cluster p-values n_permutations < 1000 (function warns 100). Tail probabilities 0.01 need permutations . Using pixel_t_test() standalone inference (§6.1). Real example (Oliveira et al., 2019, Study 1). central claim original paper Trust Friendly CIs overlap strongly Dominant Competent CIs . threshold TFCE versions cluster test, run per-producer signal matrices relevant pairs, support spatially-localized terms (FWER-controlled). full output temp/study1_replication.Rmd; runnable form : Pair rel_dissimilarity() (next section) overall-magnitude part claim.

text: . Two scalar dissimilarity metrics two conditions’ group CIs - Pearson correlation Euclidean distance - percentile bootstrap CIs. use . “different two conditions overall?” uncertainty. Pair rel_cluster_test() spatial information. Bootstrap distributions Pearson r Euclidean distance. Reading result. $correlation, $euclidean observed values; $ci_cor, $ci_dist percentile CIs; $boot_cor, $boot_dist full bootstrap distributions. Common mistakes. Reading $correlation near 1 “conditions look ” - identical spatial patterns different magnitudes give r ~ 1; magnitude difference shows $euclidean. $euclidean scales sqrt(n_pixels) within-study metric, comparable across resolutions. Real example (Oliveira et al., 2019, Study 1). Euclidean distances group-mean CIs 256x256 raw masks, 500 bootstrap replicates : Dominant-vs-Competent contrast yields substantially larger Euclidean distance Trust-vs-Friendly, direction published group-CI Pearson correlations (.69 Trust x Friendly, -.22 Dominant x Competent Table 2 paper). two metrics report pattern different angles — Pearson within-shape question, Euclidean -far-apart question. Comparing multiple contrasts side--side. several rel_dissimilarity() results want one figure shows whether CIs overlap, use compare_dissimilarity(). draws contrast horizontal point 95% CI underlying bootstrap density, reader can read overlap (non-overlap) glance. Useful paper figures.
code: diss <- rel_dissimilarity(   sig_A, sig_B,   n_boot   = 500L,   ci_level = 0.95,   seed     = 1L,   progress = FALSE ) print(diss) #> <rcicrely representational dissimilarity> #>   n_boot:               500 #>   CI level:             95% #>   n_pixels:             1024 #>  #>   Primary: Euclidean distance between group-mean CIs #>     Euclidean             = 9.668   [9.293, 10.089]   SE = 0.207 #>     Euclidean / sqrt(n)   = 0.3021   (resolution-normalised) #>  #>   [Deprecated - will be removed in v0.3] #>     Pearson r             = -0.996   [-0.994, -0.991]   SE = 0.001 #>     Note: correlation between two base-subtracted CIs has a #>     positive baseline from shared image-domain structure and is #>     not a clean similarity score. Prefer Euclidean distance. plot(diss) rel_dissimilarity(sm_trust,    sm_friendly, n_boot = 500L, seed = 1L) #> Euclidean = 16.81, 95% CI [19.26, 24.86], normalised = 0.0657 rel_dissimilarity(sm_dominant, sm_competent, n_boot = 500L, seed = 2L) #> Euclidean = 21.08, 95% CI [21.53, 30.75], normalised = 0.0823 d_TF <- rel_dissimilarity(sm_trust,    sm_friendly, n_boot = 500L, seed = 1L) d_DC <- rel_dissimilarity(sm_dominant, sm_competent, n_boot = 500L, seed = 2L) compare_dissimilarity(   "Trust vs Friendly"     = d_TF,   "Dominant vs Competent" = d_DC )

what: Troubleshooting low or negative infoval z-scores
title: rcicrely user guide
text: compute infoval() find per-producer z-scores sit well 1.96 — sometimes negative — even though spot checks suggest producers task seriously, common pattern, sign data bad. Five reasons order often apply: Frobenius norm global energy statistic. sums squared pixel deviations across entire image. Real internal representations usually spatially sparse (eyes, mouth, jaw — maybe 10-30% pixels), 70-90% “background” pixels contribute noise similar magnitude chance reference dilute signal-bearing region. producer strong, visually-obvious signal eyes can Frobenius norm marginally random reference. reference strict lives subspace. observed mask reference projections onto low-dimensional sinusoidal noise basis. reference distribution plenty overall energy construction, way clear z = 1.96 align signs specific subset patterns chance. Per-trial signal small. 2IFC choice contributes tiny signal increment relative per-trial noise amplitude. 300 trials SNR gain √300 ≈ 17×, per-trial signal ~5% per-trial noise, post-aggregation effective SNR barely visible global energy measure. Without face mask, infoVal counts background. face_mask() ships oval mask approximates Schmitz 2024 face region. Applying (infoval(..., mask =    face_mask(c(256, 256)))) concentrates norm signal-bearing pixels typically lifts z-scores noticeably. Group-level CIs much higher z individual CIs. Averaging 20 producers’ masks reduces noise √20 ≈ 4.5×, group-mean CI’s effective trial count 300 × 20 = 6000 20-producer condition. infoVal group-mean CI (trial_counts = setNames(300L * n_producers, "group")) usually 5-10× per-producer median. Brinkman et al. (2019) reported. Per-producer infoVal noisier lower; structural, defect.

text: Negative z values mean observed mask less total Frobenius energy chance reference. “negative signal” — consistent producer whose responses align structured low-rank template, since aligning consistently one template can reduce effective dimensionality mask shrink overall norm. negative z therefore informative: clearly negative z (say, < −2) producer otherwise shows good behaviour usually points one : producer responded coherently different feature trait intended (idiosyncratic representation). reference distribution mis-calibrated design (see v0.2.1 calibration note ?infoval help page — early v0.2.0 versions sampled stim ids replacement inflated reference norms, biasing per-producer z zero cooperative producers). Per-producer trial count low beat chance meaningful statistic, even real signal — fall back group-mean infoVal publishable number. Reporting recommendation. Report () group-mean infoVal z (ii) proportion producers whose individual z clears 1.96 (face mask). report median per-producer z treat values < 1.96 evidence “signal” — conflates per-producer noisiness metric group-level question whether CI carries information. Real example (Oliveira et al., 2019, Study 1, Trust condition). diagnostic recipe , run real published data. 20 individual producers shown 300 trials (trial-count-matched reference simulated 300): exactly pattern described troubleshooting list : per-producer z centred around 0.35 2 20 producers individually 1.96 threshold — yet group-mean CI z 11.39, unmistakable signal. published Trust/Friendly correlation .69 (Table 2 paper) observable trait dissociations group CIs (Figure 1 paper) consistent group-level z, reading per-producer median “weak signal” verdict. Reporting recommendation practice: “group-mean classification image showed strong informational value (z = 11.39, n_eff = 6000); 2 20 producers individually exceeded z = 1.96.” Calibration note. output reflects v0.2.1 fix simulate_reference_norms() (sample stim ids without replacement n_trials <= n_pool). v0.2.0 -inflated reference norm gave median z = -1.54 data — bug uncovered replicating Oliveira et al. (2019), Study 1. computed infoVal v0.2.0 got systematically negative z-scores, re-run upgrading.
code: sm     <- res$signal_matrix tc     <- setNames(rep(300L, ncol(sm)), colnames(sm))  # 1. Compare the observed and reference norm distributions iv  <- infoval(sm, noise_matrix, tc, iter = 1000L,                cache_path = "iv.rds", seed = 1L) ref <- iv$reference[[as.character(tc[1])]] cat(sprintf("observed median = %.4f, reference median = %.4f, %% above = %+.1f%%\n",             median(iv$norms), median(ref),             100 * (median(iv$norms) - median(ref)) / median(ref)))  # 2. Apply the face mask fm  <- face_mask(c(256L, 256L)) iv_masked <- infoval(sm, noise_matrix, tc, mask = fm,                      iter = 1000L, cache_path = "iv_masked.rds",                      seed = 1L) median(iv_masked$infoval)        # typically higher than iv  # 3. Group-mean CI (the headline number to report) group   <- matrix(rowMeans(sm), ncol = 1, dimnames = list(NULL, "group")) tc_grp  <- setNames(300L * ncol(sm), "group") iv_grp  <- infoval(group, noise_matrix, tc_grp, iter = 1000L,                    seed = 1L) iv_grp$infoval                   # usually large (e.g. > 5)  # 4. Sanity check with a simulated random responder set.seed(0) random_resp <- sample(c(-1, 1), 300L, replace = TRUE) random_mask <- (noise_matrix %*% random_resp) / 300 iv_rand <- infoval(matrix(random_mask, ncol = 1,                           dimnames = list(NULL, "rand")),                    noise_matrix,                    setNames(300L, "rand"),                    iter = 1000L, seed = 1L) iv_rand$infoval                  # should be ~ 0 within MAD noise # Per-producer infoVal tc <- setNames(rep(300L, 20L), colnames(sm_trust)) iv <- infoval(sm_trust, noise_matrix, tc, iter = 1000L, seed = 1L) #> per-producer: median z = 0.35, range [-1.69, 2.30], n above 1.96 = 2/20  # Group-mean CI infoVal (effective n_trials = 300 x 20 = 6000) group  <- matrix(rowMeans(sm_trust), ncol = 1,                  dimnames = list(NULL, "TRUST_group")) iv_grp <- infoval(group, noise_matrix,                   setNames(6000L, "TRUST_group"),                   iter = 1000L, seed = 1L) #> group-mean CI z = 11.39  # Face-mask lift on per-producer z iv_masked <- infoval(sm_trust, noise_matrix, tc,                      mask = face_mask(c(256L, 256L)),                      iter = 1000L, seed = 1L) #> masked median = 0.50  (vs unmasked median = 0.35; lift = +0.15)

Pass raw mask (res$signal_matrix) — res$rendered_ci PNG.
code: # 1. Generate the noise pool (once per project) - heavy step. rcicr::generateStimuli2IFC(   base_face_files     = list(base = "data/base.jpg"),   n_trials            = 600L,        # becomes the Brief-RC pool size   img_size            = 256L,   stimulus_path       = "data/stim",   label               = "mystudy",   ncores              = 1L,          # macOS-safe default   return_as_dataframe = TRUE,        # capture the noise matrix!   seed                = 1L ) |> saveRDS("data/noise_matrix.rds")  # cache for fast loading  # 2. Collect Brief-RC responses in the per-trial format.  # 3. Compute individual CIs. res <- ci_from_responses_briefrc(   responses       = my_brief_rc_responses,   noise_matrix    = readRDS("data/noise_matrix.rds"),   base_image_path = "data/base.jpg",   method          = "briefrc12",   scaling         = "matched"   # also rendered for plotting )  # 4. Reliability assessment. within  <- run_within(res$signal_matrix, seed = 1L) between <- run_between(res_A$signal_matrix, res_B$signal_matrix,                        seed = 1L)  # 5. Save rendered CI to PNG (visualization only - DO NOT use for stats) if (requireNamespace("png", quietly = TRUE)) {   for (j in seq_along(res$participants)) {     img <- matrix(res$rendered_ci[, j], res$img_dims[1], res$img_dims[2])     img <- pmin(pmax(img, 0), 1)     png::writePNG(img, sprintf("plots/p%02d.png", j))   } }

what: rcicrely — reliability assessment for reverse-correlation CIs
title: rcicrely — reliability assessment for reverse-correlation CIs
text: Reliability assessment reverse-correlation classification images. Within-condition consistency + -condition discriminability, without second-phase trait-rating study. rcicrely assesses reliability classification images (CIs) produced reverse-correlation experiments. answers questions infoVal doesn’t: signal CI stable, distinguishable comparison condition? Within-condition: permuted split-half Spearman–Brown correction, leave-one-sensitivity, ICC(3,1) ICC(3,k) via direct mean-squares. -condition: vectorised Welch pixel t, cluster-based permutation max-statistic FWER control, bootstrap representational dissimilarity. Supports standard 2IFC (via canonical rcicr) Brief-RC 12 (Schmitz, Rougier & Yzerbyt, 2024; implemented natively). Companion rcicrdiagnostics: diagnostics catches silent data-processing errors CI computation; rcicrely asks whether CIs reliable discriminable cleanly computed.

Row count must match signal_matrix. trial_counts Named integer vector trial counts per producer. Names must match colnames(signal_matrix). iter Reference-distribution Monte Carlo size. Default 10000 (matches rcicr convention). mask Optional logical vector length nrow(signal_matrix). supplied, observed reference norms computed masked pixel subset. cache_path Optional path .rds file. set file exists, reference distributions loaded (keyed trial count); set file exist, computed distributions written running. accelerates repeated runs (noise_matrix, iter, mask, seed) configuration. cache stores reference norms, never observed data. seed Optional integer; RNG state restored exit. progress Show cli progress bar.

text: Object class rcicrely_infoval fields: $infoval, per-producer z-scores (named numeric vector). $norms, per-producer observed Frobenius norms. $reference, named list reference norm vectors, keyed trial count. $ref_median, $ref_mad, named numeric vectors (centre / spread) per trial count. $trial_counts, $mask, $iter, $n_pool, $seed, metadata.

title: Per-producer informational value with trial-count-matched reference — infoval
text: observed statistic Frobenius norm producer j's classification image mask, optionally restricted logical mask (e.g. oval face region via face_mask()):   reference distribution producer j built simulating iter random masks trial count trial_counts[j], mirroring genMask() construction exactly (Schmitz, Rougier, & Yzerbyt, 2024): Sample trial_counts[j] stimulus ids uniformly 1:n_pool replacement. Sample trial_counts[j] responses uniformly {-1, +1}. Collapse response stim via mean() (duplicate-stim rule used Brief-RC). Build mask: noise_matrix[, unique_stims] %*% mean_response / n_unique_stims. Apply mask (supplied) compute Frobenius norm. per-producer z-score (norm_j - median(ref)) / mad(ref), reference distribution one matched producer j's trial count. Producers sharing trial count share reference; makes simulation efficient several producers number trials. Choice statistic: Frobenius norm correct form per Schmitz, Muller, & Yzerbyt (2019, comment) 2020 erratum. Canonical rcicr::computeInfoVal2IFC() already uses (norm(matrix(target_ci[["ci"]]), "f")); function matches convention 2IFC Brief-RC data. text: producer , compute Pearson correlation full-sample group CI group CI recomputed without producer:   full-sample mean leave-one-mean share (N - 1) / N data, r_loo values near 1 construction even noisy data, typically [0.95, 0.999] N = 30. absolute level r_loo informative. informative relative ordering: producers whose r_loo sits clearly pack candidates inspection. reason function also returns z-scored version r_loo $z_scores, using centre / spread estimators flagging rule. $z_scores recommended quantity plot report. Two flagging rules: "sd" (default): flag producers r_loo < mean(r) - flag_threshold * sd(r), equivalently z_scores < -flag_threshold. Standard convention; sensitive outliers trying detect, flagging threshold can pulled outliers' region. "mad": flag producers r_loo < median(r) - flag_threshold * mad(r), equivalently z_scores < -flag_threshold robust centre/spread. Robust atypical producers typically show RC datasets skewed correlation distributions. default flag_threshold = 2.5 calibrated 30-producer dataset flags roughly 0.3 producers chance "sd", rather ~1.5 2-SD rule produce. "mad" roughly comparable thanks MAD's 1.4826 consistency factor.

what: Permuted split-half reliability with Spearman-Brown correction — rel_split_half
title: Permuted split-half reliability with Spearman-Brown correction — rel_split_half
text: Measures stable condition's group-level CI across random halves producer set. n_permutations iterations: randomly partition producers (columns signal_matrix) two roughly-equal halves, average half get two group vectors, compute Pearson correlation. Use answer "get group CI run different half participants?". odd N, one randomly-chosen producer dropped per permutation (re-drawn iteration, fixed) halves floor(N/2) producers. Reports mean per-permutation r Spearman-Brown corrected reliability r_SB = (2 r) / (1 + r). 95% CI 2.5 / 97.5 percentile permutation distribution.

what: Raw vs. rendered CIs - documentation overhaul + safety net
title: rcicrely 0.1.1 (unreleased)
text: Reliability metrics operate raw mask (participant’s unscaled noise contribution). Reading PNGs disk via read_cis() silently loaded rendered CI (base + scaling(mask)) fed scaling(mask) downstream metrics, can distort variance-based statistics (rel_icc(), Euclidean dissimilarity, pixel_t_test(), rel_cluster_test()). canonical 2IFC infoVal path (rcicr::computeInfoVal2IFC()) unaffected extracts raw $ci element rcicr CI-list internally; hand-rolled infoVal implementations (Brief-RC, custom code) affected need raw mask. mathematical core package always correct; release sharpens boundary users see issue. read_cis(), extract_signal(), load_signal_matrix() now emit -per-session cli warning explaining raw-vs-rendered distinction, metrics affected, silence (acknowledge_scaling = TRUE argument options(rcicrely.silence_scaling_warning = TRUE)). Every rel_*() run_*() function now runs cheap heuristic entry (looks_scaled()) emits -per-session warning input matrix’s dynamic range looks rendered rather raw. Heuristic ; silencing options. vignette("tutorial") restructured function--function user guide (~600 lines, ~350). Adds dedicated “Mental model: signal matrix” chapter numerical demonstration scaling distorts variance-based metrics leaving Pearson correlation intact, “Interpreting results” chapter flags infoVal pitfall. README.md adds top-level “Important: raw vs. rendered CIs” callout.

Mirrors ci_from_responses_2ifc()’s new keep_rendered = TRUE argument, adds $rendered_ci field extracted rcicr’s $combined.

title: rcicrely 0.1.1 (unreleased)

"mad" flags producers whose LOO correlation falls median(r) - flag_threshold * mad(r), robust atypical producers often dominate SD rule. flag_threshold_sd argument renamed flag_threshold (multiplier applies either SD MAD). old name still works alias existing code break. Result object gains $median_r, $mad_r, $flag_method, $flag_threshold fields.

title: rcicrely 0.1.0
text: rel_split_half() - permuted split-half Spearman-Brown correction; handles odd N via per-permutation drop. rel_loo() - leave-one-sensitivity 2.5-SD outlier flagging default. rel_icc() - ICC(3,1) ICC(3,k) via direct mean-squares (two-way mixed model: pixels fixed, participants random); ICC(2,*) available request. pixel_t_test() - vectorised Welch’s t per pixel. rel_cluster_test() - cluster-based permutation test 4-connectivity labelling max-statistic FWER control; permutation stratified (preserves N_A, N_B). rel_dissimilarity() - bootstrap correlation + Euclidean distance percentile CIs (boot dependency). run_within(), run_between() - run every metric, wrap rcicrely_report. read_cis(), extract_signal(), load_signal_matrix() pre-computed CI images. read_noise_matrix() rcicr .Rdata plain-text noise matrices. ci_from_responses_2ifc() - wraps rcicr::batchGenerateCI2IFC() known gotchas handled. ci_from_responses_briefrc() - native Brief-RC 12 implementation per Schmitz, Rougier & Yzerbyt (2024), matching genMask() exactly including duplicate-stimulus tie-breaking. S3 methods - print, summary, plot every rcicrely_* result class plus rcicrely_report.
